topic: Adaptive orchestration switching in LLM multi-agent systems: dynamically selecting between orchestration patterns based on task characteristics at runtime
search_queries: all:adaptive AND all:orchestration AND all:multi-agent AND all:LLM
all:dynamic AND all:agent AND all:topology AND all:LLM
all:LLM AND all:routing AND all:cost AND all:optimization
all:multi-agent AND all:workflow AND all:optimization AND all:language
all:hierarchical AND all:multi-agent AND all:planning AND all:LLM
all:task AND all:decomposition AND all:agent AND all:orchestration
key_paper: Multi-Agent Collaboration via Evolving Orchestration

papers.title: AFlow: Automating Agentic Workflow Generation
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable potential in solving complex tasks across diverse domains, typically by employing agentic workflows that follow detailed instructions and operational sequences. However, constructing these workflows requires significant human effort, limiting scalability and generalizability. Recent research has sought to automate the generation and optimization of these workflows, but existing methods still rely on initial manual setup and fall short of
papers.contribution: Reformulates agentic workflow optimization as search over code-represented workflows using Monte Carlo Tree Search, achieving 5.7% average improvement over SOTA. ICLR 2025 Oral.
papers.limitation: MCTS search is computationally expensive; discovered workflows may overfit to specific benchmark distributions.

papers.title: Aragog: Just-in-Time Model Routing for Scalable Serving of Agentic Workflows
papers.abstract: Agentic workflows have emerged as a powerful paradigm for solving complex, multi-stage tasks, but serving them at scale is computationally expensive given the many LLM inferences that each request must pass through. Configuration selection, or the cost-aware assignment of workflow agents to specific LLMs, can reduce these costs, but existing approaches bind configuration decisions before request execution, making them ill-suited for the heterogeneous and lengthy execution of workflows. Specifica
papers.contribution: Proposes just-in-time model routing for serving agentic workflows at scale, dynamically allocating compute resources based on workflow stage requirements.
papers.limitation: System-level optimization focus; does not address the higher-level question of which orchestration pattern to use.

papers.title: Trust by Design: Skill Profiles for Transparent, Cost-Aware LLM Routing
papers.abstract: How should Large Language Model (LLM) practitioners select the right model for a task without wasting money? We introduce BELLA (Budget-Efficient LLM Selection via Automated skill-profiling), a framework that recommends optimal LLM selection for tasks through interpretable skill-based model selection. Standard benchmarks report aggregate metrics that obscure which specific capabilities a task requires and whether a cheaper model could suffice. BELLA addresses this gap through three stages: (1) d
papers.contribution: Introduces skill profiles for transparent LLM routing, enabling cost-aware selection based on explicitly modeled model capabilities per task type.
papers.limitation: Skill profiles require careful manual or automated construction; may not capture emergent model capabilities.

papers.title: AdaptOrch: Task-Adaptive Multi-Agent Orchestration in the Era of LLM Performance Convergence
papers.abstract: As large language models from diverse providers converge toward comparable benchmark performance, the traditional paradigm of selecting a single best model per task yields diminishing returns. We argue that orchestration topology -- the structural composition of how multiple agents are coordinated, parallelized, and synthesized -- now dominates system-level performance over individual model capability. We present AdaptOrch, a formal framework for task-adaptive multi-agent orchestration that dyna
papers.contribution: Formalizes task-adaptive multi-agent orchestration with a framework that dynamically selects among four canonical topologies (parallel, sequential, hierarchical, hybrid) based on task dependency graphs, demonstrating that orchestration topology dominates performance over individual model capability.
papers.limitation: Evaluation limited to a fixed set of four topology patterns; does not explore continuous topology spaces or mixed/hybrid patterns within a single task execution.

papers.title: Difficulty-Aware Agentic Orchestration for Query-Specific Multi-Agent Workflows
papers.abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agentic systems have shown strong capabilities across various tasks. However, existing multi-agent frameworks often rely on static or task-level workflows, which either over-process simple queries or underperform on complex ones, while also neglecting the efficiency-performance trade-offs across heterogeneous LLMs. To address these limitations, we propose Difficulty-Aware Agentic Orchestration (DAAO), which can dynamically generate query-specific multi-agent work
papers.contribution: Presents DAAO, a dynamic framework adapting workflow depth, operator selection, and LLM assignment based on query difficulty, using a VAE for difficulty estimation combined with cost-performance-aware LLM routing.
papers.limitation: Difficulty estimation via VAE adds latency overhead; assumes difficulty is estimable from the query alone without task execution feedback.

papers.title: Cost-Aware Agentic Architectures for Multi-Model Routing and Tool-Use Optimisation in CRM Workflows
papers.venue: International Journal of Leading Research Publication
papers.abstract: The study presents research into the application of cost-conscious agentic support in the field of multi-model routing and tool-use optimization to the CRM post-sale processes. It is focused on how to dynamically dispatch tasks to suitable model tiers (small, medium, large LLMs) with a view to the minimum expense of API services and high software quality. The study aims to examine how Salesforce Service Cloud can be used to carry out post-sales CRM activities, including case escalation and email
papers.contribution: Evaluates cost-aware architectural choices for multi-model routing and tool-use optimization in real-world CRM settings, providing practical guidelines for deployment.
papers.limitation: Focused on CRM domain; findings may not directly transfer to general-purpose agent orchestration.

papers.title: Optimizing Agentic Workflows using Meta-tools
papers.abstract: Agentic AI enables LLM to dynamically reason, plan, and interact with tools to solve complex tasks. However, agentic workflows often require many iterative reasoning steps and tool invocations, leading to significant operational expense, end-to-end latency and failures due to hallucinations. This work introduces Agent Workflow Optimization (AWO), a framework that identifies and optimizes redundant tool execution patterns to improve the efficiency and robustness of agentic workflows. AWO analyzes
papers.contribution: Proposes meta-tools as reusable workflow components that can be composed and optimized to build efficient agentic workflows.
papers.limitation: Meta-tool abstraction may not capture all workflow patterns; composition rules require manual specification.

papers.title: CASTER: Breaking the Cost-Performance Barrier in Multi-Agent Orchestration via Context-Aware Strategy for Task Efficient Routing
papers.abstract: Graph-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) enable complex cyclic workflows but suffer from inefficient static model allocation, where deploying strong models uniformly wastes computation on trivial sub-tasks. We propose CASTER (Context-Aware Strategy for Task Efficient Routing), a lightweight router for dynamic model selection in graph-based MAS. CASTER employs a Dual-Signal Router that combines semantic embeddings with structural meta-features to estimate task difficulty. During training, the router
papers.contribution: Introduces a dual-signal router combining semantic embeddings with structural meta-features for dynamic model selection in graph-based multi-agent systems, achieving up to 72.4% cost reduction while matching strong-model baselines.
papers.limitation: Router training requires domain-specific calibration data; cold-start performance on unseen task domains is not thoroughly evaluated.

papers.title: Dynamic Model Routing and Cascading for Efficient LLM Inference: A Survey
papers.abstract: The rapid growth of large language models (LLMs) with diverse capabilities, costs, and domains has created a critical need for intelligent model selection at inference time. While smaller models suffice for routine queries, complex tasks demand more capable models. However, static model deployment does not account for the complexity and domain of incoming queries, leading to suboptimal performance and increased costs. Dynamic routing systems that adaptively select models based on query character
papers.contribution: Provides a comprehensive survey of model routing and cascading techniques for efficient LLM inference, cataloging approaches from simple thresholding to learned routers.
papers.limitation: Survey paper; does not propose new methods. Coverage of multi-agent routing is limited.

papers.title: One Head, Many Models: Cross-Attention Routing for Cost-Aware LLM Selection
papers.abstract: The proliferation of large language models (LLMs) with varying computational costs and performance profiles presents a critical challenge for scalable, cost-effective deployment in real-world applications. We introduce a unified routing framework that leverages a single-head cross-attention mechanism to jointly model query and model embeddings, enabling dynamic selection of the optimal LLM for each input query. Our approach is evaluated on RouterBench, a large-scale, publicly available benchmark
papers.contribution: Uses a cross-attention mechanism as a routing head that learns to select among multiple LLMs based on input features, enabling cost-aware model selection.
papers.limitation: Cross-attention head adds inference overhead; requires training data from all candidate models.

papers.title: xRouter: Training Cost-Aware LLMs Orchestration System via Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Modern LLM deployments confront a widening cost-performance spectrum: premium models deliver strong reasoning but are expensive, while lightweight models are economical yet brittle on complex tasks. Static escalation rules and keyword heuristics under-utilize this spectrum and fail to adapt across task types. We present xRouter, a tool-calling-based routing system in which a learned router can either answer directly or invoke one or more external models. The router is trained end-to-end with rei
papers.contribution: Uses reinforcement learning with cost-aware reward shaping to train an intelligent router across 20+ LLMs, achieving near-GPT-5 accuracy at ~12.5% of its cost. From Salesforce AI Research.
papers.limitation: Router backbone (Qwen2.5-7B) adds significant compute; requires retraining when new models are added to the pool.

papers.title: Multi-Agent Design: Optimizing Agents with Better Prompts and Topologies
papers.abstract: Large language models, employed as multiple agents that interact and collaborate with each other, have excelled at solving complex tasks. The agents are programmed with prompts that declare their functionality, along with the topologies that orchestrate interactions across agents. Designing prompts and topologies for multi-agent systems (MAS) is inherently complex. To automate the entire design process, we first conduct an in-depth analysis of the design space aiming to understand the factors be
papers.contribution: Proposes MASS framework interleaving local-to-global optimization of prompts and topologies across three stages, demonstrating that joint prompt-topology optimization substantially outperforms alternatives. Under review at ICLR 2026.
papers.limitation: Optimization is offline/design-time; does not adapt topology at runtime based on individual query characteristics.

papers.title: Adaptive Orchestration: Scalable Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Systems
papers.abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents, they face a critical scalability bottleneck known as the"Generalization-Specialization Dilemma."Monolithic agents equipped with extensive toolkits suffer from context pollution and attention decay, leading to hallucinations. Conversely, static multi-agent swarms introduce significant latency and resource overhead. This paper introduces a Self-Evolving Concierge System, a novel architecture utilizing a Dynamic Mixture
papers.contribution: Proposes a self-evolving multi-agent architecture that automatically scales and reorganizes agent teams based on task demands, without requiring predefined orchestration patterns.
papers.limitation: Scalability claims lack rigorous benchmarking against established multi-agent frameworks on standardized tasks.

papers.title: Difficulty-Aware Agent Orchestration in LLM-Powered Workflows
papers.contribution: Proposes difficulty-aware agent orchestration that adjusts agent pipeline complexity based on input difficulty, reducing unnecessary computation for simple queries.
papers.limitation: Overlaps significantly with concurrent DAAO work; distinction between the two approaches is unclear.

papers.title: EvoFlow: Evolving Diverse Agentic Workflows On The Fly
papers.abstract: The past two years have witnessed the evolution of large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems from labor-intensive manual design to partial automation (\textit{e.g.}, prompt engineering, communication topology) and eventually to fully automated design. However, existing agentic automation pipelines often lack LLM heterogeneity and focus on single-objective performance optimization, limiting their potential to combine weaker models for more customized and cost-effective solutions. To ad
papers.contribution: Proposes evolutionary methods to discover diverse agentic workflows on the fly, generating varied workflow structures through mutation and crossover operations.
papers.limitation: Evolutionary search requires many evaluations; diversity pressure may sacrifice peak performance for exploration.

papers.title: FlowSteer: Interactive Agentic Workflow Orchestration via End-to-End Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: In recent years, a variety of powerful agentic workflows have been applied to solve a wide range of human problems. However, existing workflow orchestration still faces key challenges, including high manual cost, reliance on specific operators/large language models (LLMs), and sparse reward signals. To address these challenges, we propose FlowSteer, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that takes a lightweight policy model as the agent and an executable canvas environment, automating w
papers.contribution: Enables interactive agentic workflow orchestration via end-to-end reinforcement learning, allowing human-guided steering of workflow execution.
papers.limitation: Human-in-the-loop requirement limits full autonomy; RL training requires extensive interaction data.

papers.title: Towards Efficient Multi-LLM Inference: Characterization and Analysis of LLM Routing and Hierarchical Techniques
papers.abstract: Recent progress in Language Models (LMs) has dramatically advanced the field of natural language processing (NLP), excelling at tasks like text generation, summarization, and question answering. However, their inference remains computationally expensive and energy intensive, especially in settings with limited hardware, power, or bandwidth. This makes it difficult to deploy LMs in mobile, edge, or cost sensitive environments. To address these challenges, recent approaches have introduced multi L
papers.contribution: Characterizes and analyzes LLM routing and hierarchical inference patterns, providing empirical insights into when routing provides benefits over single-model approaches.
papers.limitation: Empirical characterization without a unified framework; findings may not generalize beyond evaluated benchmarks.

papers.title: Bayesian Orchestration of Multi-LLM Agents for Cost-Aware Sequential Decision-Making
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous decision agents in settings with asymmetric error costs: hiring (missed talent vs wasted interviews), medical triage (missed emergencies vs unnecessary escalation), and fraud detection (approved fraud vs declined legitimate payments). The dominant design queries a single LLM for a posterior over states, thresholds"confidence,"and acts; we prove this is inadequate for sequential decisions with costs. We propose a Bayesian, cost-
papers.contribution: Treats LLMs as approximate likelihood models in a Bayesian framework for cost-aware sequential decision-making, enabling coherent belief updating and expected-cost action selection across multiple LLMs.
papers.limitation: Bayesian inference adds computational overhead; assumes well-specified priors which may not hold for novel task distributions.

papers.title: Cost-Aware Contrastive Routing for LLMs
papers.abstract: We study cost-aware routing for large language models across diverse and dynamic pools of models. Existing approaches often overlook prompt-specific context, rely on expensive model profiling, assume a fixed set of experts, or use inefficient trial-and-error strategies. We introduce Cost-Spectrum Contrastive Routing (CSCR), a lightweight framework that maps both prompts and models into a shared embedding space to enable fast, cost-sensitive selection. CSCR uses compact, fast-to-compute logit foo
papers.contribution: Applies contrastive learning to train cost-aware routing policies for LLMs, learning to distinguish between queries that require expensive vs. cheap models.
papers.limitation: Contrastive training requires paired examples of easy/hard queries; may not capture fine-grained difficulty gradients.

papers.title: FrugalGPT: How to Use Large Language Models While Reducing Cost and Improving Performance
papers.abstract: There is a rapidly growing number of large language models (LLMs) that users can query for a fee. We review the cost associated with querying popular LLM APIs, e.g. GPT-4, ChatGPT, J1-Jumbo, and find that these models have heterogeneous pricing structures, with fees that can differ by two orders of magnitude. In particular, using LLMs on large collections of queries and text can be expensive. Motivated by this, we outline and discuss three types of strategies that users can exploit to reduce the
papers.contribution: Pioneering work on LLM cost optimization through cascade strategies that route queries through progressively more expensive models, achieving up to 98% cost reduction with minimal quality loss.
papers.limitation: Cascade is sequential and adds latency; quality estimation relies on simple scoring functions that may not generalize across task types.

papers.title: RouteLLM: Learning to Route LLMs from Preference Data
papers.contribution: Trains a learned router using preference data to dynamically select between strong and weak LLMs per query, achieving significant cost savings with controllable quality thresholds.
papers.limitation: Binary routing (strong vs. weak) is limiting; does not extend to routing across many models or multi-agent configurations.

papers.title: Orchestrating Intelligence: Confidence-Aware Routing for Efficient Multi-Agent Collaboration across Multi-Scale Models
papers.abstract: While multi-agent systems (MAS) have demonstrated superior performance over single-agent approaches in complex reasoning tasks, they often suffer from significant computational inefficiencies. Existing frameworks typically deploy large language models (LLMs) uniformly across all agent roles, failing to account for the varying cognitive demands of different reasoning stages. We address this inefficiency by proposing OI-MAS framework, a novel multi-agent framework that implements an adaptive model
papers.contribution: Proposes OI-MAS with confidence-aware routing that dynamically selects agent roles and model scales throughout reasoning, improving accuracy by up to 12.88% while reducing cost by up to 79.78%.
papers.limitation: Confidence calibration assumes well-calibrated model outputs; performance degrades when base models produce poorly calibrated confidence scores.

papers.title: AOI: Context-Aware Multi-Agent Operations via Dynamic Scheduling and Hierarchical Memory Compression
papers.abstract: The proliferation of cloud-native architectures, characterized by microservices and dynamic orchestration, has rendered modern IT infrastructures exceedingly complex and volatile. This complexity generates overwhelming volumes of operational data, leading to critical bottlenecks in conventional systems: inefficient information processing, poor task coordination, and loss of contextual continuity during fault diagnosis and remediation. To address these challenges, we propose AOI (AI-Oriented Oper
papers.contribution: Proposes context-aware multi-agent operations with dynamic scheduling and hierarchical memory coordination for adaptive agent execution.
papers.limitation: Dynamic scheduling adds overhead; hierarchical memory coordination complexity grows with agent count.

papers.title: Multi-agent Architecture Search via Agentic Supernet
papers.abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-empowered multi-agent systems extend the cognitive boundaries of individual agents through disciplined collaboration and interaction, while constructing these systems often requires labor-intensive manual designs. Despite the availability of methods to automate the design of agentic workflows, they typically seek to identify a static, complex, one-size-fits-all system, which, however, fails to dynamically allocate inference resources based on the difficulty and domain
papers.contribution: Introduces MaAS, an agentic supernet approach that generates query-specific multi-agent systems by searching over agent configurations, achieving adaptive agent composition per input.
papers.limitation: Search overhead at inference time; the supernet approach requires pre-training across many agent configurations.

papers.title: Budget-Aware Agentic Routing via Boundary-Guided Training
papers.abstract: As large language models (LLMs) evolve into autonomous agents that execute long-horizon workflows, invoking a high-capability model at every step becomes economically unsustainable. While model routing is effective for single-turn queries, agentic routing is a sequential, path-dependent problem: early mistakes compound, feedback is often at the end of the episode, and deployments often demand strict per-task spending limits. We propose Budget-Aware Agentic Routing, which selects between a cheap
papers.contribution: Proposes boundary-guided training for learning routing policies under explicit budget constraints, enabling Pareto-optimal cost-quality tradeoffs.
papers.limitation: Budget specification requires domain knowledge; does not handle dynamic budget adjustments during execution.

papers.title: Weak-for-Strong: Training Weak Meta-Agent to Harness Strong Executors
papers.abstract: Efficiently leveraging of the capabilities of contemporary large language models (LLMs) is increasingly challenging, particularly when direct fine-tuning is expensive and often impractical. Existing training-free methods, including manually or automated designed workflows, typically demand substantial human effort or yield suboptimal results. This paper proposes Weak-for-Strong Harnessing (W4S), a novel framework that customizes smaller, cost-efficient language models to design and optimize work
papers.contribution: Trains a weak meta-agent to orchestrate strong executor models, demonstrating that a small routing model can effectively harness larger models for complex tasks.
papers.limitation: Meta-agent capacity limits the complexity of routing decisions; performance ceiling tied to the weak model's understanding.

papers.title: Toward Super Agent System with Hybrid AI Routers
papers.abstract: AI Agents powered by Large Language Models are transforming the world through enormous applications. A super agent has the potential to fulfill diverse user needs, such as summarization, coding, and research, by accurately understanding user intent and leveraging the appropriate tools to solve tasks. However, to make such an agent viable for real-world deployment and accessible at scale, significant optimizations are required to ensure high efficiency and low cost. This position paper presents a
papers.contribution: Proposes hybrid AI routers that combine multiple routing strategies (learned, rule-based, ensemble) for selecting among agent capabilities.
papers.limitation: Hybrid approach increases system complexity; interaction between routing strategies is not well characterized.

papers.title: LLMs Working in Harmony: A Survey on the Technological Aspects of Building Effective LLM-Based Multi Agent Systems
papers.abstract: This survey investigates foundational technologies essential for developing effective Large Language Model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems. Aiming to answer how best to optimize these systems for collaborative, dynamic environments, we focus on four critical areas: Architecture, Memory, Planning, and Technologies/Frameworks. By analyzing recent advancements and their limitations - such as scalability, real-time response challenges, and agent coordination constraints, we provide a detailed view o
papers.contribution: Surveys technological aspects of building effective LLM-based multi-agent systems, covering communication, coordination, and system design principles.
papers.limitation: Primarily descriptive; lacks quantitative analysis of when different coordination patterns are optimal.

papers.title: Multi-Agent Collaboration Mechanisms: A Survey of LLMs
papers.abstract: With recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs), Agentic AI has become phenomenal in real-world applications, moving toward multiple LLM-based agents to perceive, learn, reason, and act collaboratively. These LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems (MASs) enable groups of intelligent agents to coordinate and solve complex tasks collectively at scale, transitioning from isolated models to collaboration-centric approaches. This work provides an extensive survey of the collaborative aspect of MASs and i
papers.contribution: Provides a taxonomy of collaboration mechanisms in LLM multi-agent systems including debate, reflection, and role specialization, analyzing when each mechanism is effective.
papers.limitation: Focuses on collaboration mechanisms rather than meta-level orchestration selection; does not address cost-quality tradeoffs in mechanism choice.

papers.title: HAWK: A Hierarchical Workflow Framework for Multi-Agent Collaboration
papers.abstract: Contemporary multi-agent systems encounter persistent challenges in cross-platform interoperability, dynamic task scheduling, and efficient resource sharing. Agents with heterogeneous implementations often lack standardized interfaces; collaboration frameworks remain brittle and hard to extend; scheduling policies are static; and inter-agent state synchronization is insufficient. We propose Hierarchical Agent Workflow (HAWK), a modular framework comprising five layers-User, Workflow, Operator, A
papers.contribution: Introduces a hierarchical workflow framework for multi-agent collaboration, organizing agents in tree-structured workflows with explicit delegation and aggregation patterns.
papers.limitation: Fixed hierarchical structure; does not support dynamic reorganization based on task characteristics or agent performance.

papers.title: AgentVerse: Facilitating Multi-Agent Collaboration and Exploring Emergent Behaviors in Agents
papers.abstract: Autonomous agents empowered by Large Language Models (LLMs) have undergone significant improvements, enabling them to generalize across a broad spectrum of tasks. However, in real-world scenarios, cooperation among individuals is often required to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of task accomplishment. Hence, inspired by human group dynamics, we propose a multi-agent framework \framework that can collaboratively and dynamically adjust its composition as a greater-than-the-sum-of-its-par
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that dynamically adjusting agent group composition during task solving leads to better performance than static assignments, with emergent specialization patterns. ICLR 2024.
papers.limitation: Agent group dynamics are rule-based; scaling beyond 10 agents shows diminishing returns.

papers.title: MetaGPT: Meta Programming for Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework
papers.abstract: Recently, remarkable progress has been made in automated task-solving through the use of multi-agent driven by large language models (LLMs). However, existing LLM-based multi-agent works primarily focus on solving simple dialogue tasks, and complex tasks are rarely studied, mainly due to the LLM hallucination problem. This type of hallucination becomes cascading when naively chaining multiple intelligent agents, resulting in a failure to effectively address complex problems. Therefore, we introd
papers.contribution: Encodes human-like SOPs into multi-agent collaboration, assigning specialized roles with structured outputs to reduce hallucination and improve software development task completion.
papers.limitation: Role assignments and workflow are static; the SOP-based approach requires domain-specific engineering for each new task type.

papers.title: MasRouter: Learning to Route LLMs for Multi-Agent Systems
papers.venue: Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
papers.abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) have been demonstrated to push the boundaries of LLM capabilities, yet they often incur significant costs and face challenges in dynamic LLM selection. Current LLM routing methods effectively reduce overhead in single-agent scenarios by customizing LLM selection for each query, but they overlook the critical decisions regarding collaboration modes and agent roles in MAS. In response to this challenge, we first introduce the proble
papers.contribution: Introduces Multi-Agent System Routing (MASR) integrating collaboration mode determination, role allocation, and LLM routing in a unified cascaded controller. Published at ACL 2025.
papers.limitation: Cascaded controller adds inference overhead; routing decisions are made upfront without mid-execution adaptation.

papers.title: AnyMAC: Cascading Flexible Multi-Agent Collaboration via Next-Agent Prediction
papers.venue: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
papers.abstract: Recent progress in large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent collaboration highlights the power of structured communication in enabling collective intelligence. However, existing methods largely rely on static or graph-based inter-agent topologies, lacking the potential adaptability and flexibility in communication. In this work, we propose a new framework that rethinks multi-agent coordination through a sequential structure rather than a graph structure, offering a significantly larger topol
papers.contribution: Rethinks multi-agent coordination through sequential structure rather than graph structure, using next-agent prediction and next-context selection to construct task-adaptive communication pipelines. Published at EMNLP 2025.
papers.limitation: Sequential formulation may miss beneficial parallel execution opportunities; pipeline depth grows linearly with task complexity.

papers.title: Topological Structure Learning Should Be A Research Priority for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
papers.abstract: Large Language Model-based Multi-Agent Systems (MASs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for tackling complex tasks through collaborative intelligence. However, the topology of these systems--how agents in MASs should be configured, connected, and coordinated--remains largely unexplored. In this position paper, we call for a paradigm shift toward \emph{topology-aware MASs} that explicitly model and dynamically optimize the structure of inter-agent interactions. We identify three fundamental com
papers.contribution: Presents a systematic three-stage framework (agent selection, structure profiling, topology synthesis) arguing that topology-aware MAS design should be a research priority, with up to 10% performance variation across topologies.
papers.limitation: Position/survey paper; framework is conceptual without end-to-end implementation. Combinatorial design space remains computationally prohibitive.

papers.title: Graph of Thoughts: Solving Elaborate Problems with Large Language Models
papers.abstract:
We introduce Graph of Thoughts (GoT): a framework that
advances prompting capabilities in large language models
(LLMs) beyond those offered by paradigms such as 
Chain-of-Thought or Tree of Thoughts (ToT). The key idea and 
primary advantage of GoT is the ability to model the information 
generated by an LLM as an arbitrary graph, where units of 
information ("LLM thoughts") are vertices, and edges correspond
to dependencies between these vertices. This approach enables 
combining arbitrary LLM
papers.contribution: Introduces a graph-based reasoning framework that generalizes chain and tree paradigms, enabling arbitrary graph structures for LLM problem-solving with operations like aggregation and refinement.
papers.limitation: Graph construction requires task-specific engineering; automated graph topology discovery is not addressed.

papers.title: Towards Adaptive, Scalable, and Robust Coordination of LLM Agents: A Dynamic Ad-Hoc Networking Perspective
papers.abstract: Multi-agent architectures built on large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated the potential to realize swarm intelligence through well-crafted collaboration. However, the substantial burden of manual orchestration inherently raises an imperative to automate the design of agentic workflows. We frame such an agent coordination challenge as a classic problem in dynamic ad-hoc networking: How to establish adaptive and reliable communication among a scalable number of agentic hosts? In response t
papers.contribution: Argues that prevailing MAS orchestration is analogous to legacy circuit-switching networks and proposes a paradigm shift to content-centric packet-switching where routing decisions are decentralized and driven by semantic content.
papers.limitation: Primarily a position/vision paper; lacks comprehensive empirical validation of the proposed networking-inspired architecture.

papers.title: AQA: Adaptive Question Answering in a Society of LLMs via Contextual Multi-Armed Bandit
papers.abstract: In question answering (QA), different questions can be effectively addressed with different answering strategies. Some require a simple lookup, while others need complex, multi-step reasoning to be answered adequately. This observation motivates the development of a dynamic method that adaptively selects the most suitable QA strategy for each question, enabling more efficient and effective systems capable of addressing a broader range of question types. To this aim, we build on recent advances i
papers.contribution: Formulates adaptive question answering in multi-LLM societies as a contextual multi-armed bandit problem, learning online which LLM to query for each question type.
papers.limitation: Bandit formulation assumes stationary reward distributions; does not account for LLM capability changes over time or multi-step reasoning.

papers.title: Multi-Agent Collaboration via Evolving Orchestration
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable results across diverse downstream tasks, but their monolithic nature restricts scalability and efficiency in complex problem-solving. While recent research explores multi-agent collaboration among LLMs, most approaches rely on static organizational structures that struggle to adapt as task complexity and agent numbers grow, resulting in coordination overhead and inefficiencies. To this end, we propose a puppeteer-style paradigm for LLM-based
papers.contribution: Introduces a puppeteer-style paradigm where a centralized orchestrator trained via reinforcement learning dynamically sequences and prioritizes agents, achieving implicit inference graph construction. Accepted at NeurIPS 2025.
papers.limitation: Centralized orchestrator creates a single point of failure; RL training requires substantial compute and may not transfer across task domains.

papers.title: AgentConductor: Topology Evolution for Multi-Agent Competition-Level Code Generation
papers.abstract: Large language model(LLM)-driven multi-agent systems(MAS) coordinate specialized agents through predefined interaction topologies and have shown promise for complex tasks such as competition-level code generation. Recent studies demonstrate that carefully designed multi-agent workflows and communication graphs can significantly improve code generation performance by leveraging collaborative reasoning. However, existing methods neither adapt topology density to task difficulty nor iteratively ref
papers.contribution: Proposes topology evolution for multi-agent code generation, dynamically adjusting inter-agent communication patterns during competitive code synthesis.
papers.limitation: Evaluated only on code generation tasks; generalizability to other domains is unclear.

papers.title: Orchestrating Agents and Data for Enterprise: A Blueprint Architecture for Compound AI
papers.venue: 2025 IEEE 41st International Conference on Data Engineering Workshops (ICDEW)
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have gained significant interest in industry due to their impressive capabilities across a wide range of tasks. However, the widespread adoption of LLMs presents several challenges, such as integration into existing applications and infrastructure, utilization of company proprietary data, models, and APIs, and meeting cost, quality, responsiveness, and other requirements. To address these challenges, there is a notable shift from monolithic models to compound AI syst
papers.contribution: Proposes a blueprint architecture for compound AI systems in enterprise settings, covering agent orchestration, data integration, and deployment patterns.
papers.limitation: Enterprise-focused blueprint; does not provide empirical evaluation of orchestration effectiveness.

papers.title: AgentSwift: Efficient LLM Agent Design via Value-guided Hierarchical Search
papers.abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents have demonstrated strong capabilities across diverse domains, yet automated agent design remains a significant challenge. Current automated agent design approaches are often constrained by limited search spaces that primarily optimize workflows but fail to integrate crucial human-designed components like memory, planning, and tool use. Furthermore, these methods are hampered by high evaluation costs, as evaluating even a single new agent on a benchmark can requi
papers.contribution: Proposes efficient LLM agent design via value-guided hierarchical search over agent configurations, finding cost-effective agent setups.
papers.limitation: Search procedure requires significant offline compute; runtime adaptation is limited.

papers.title: A Survey on Agent Workflow – Status and Future
papers.venue: 2025 8th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Big Data (ICAIBD)
papers.abstract: In the age of large language models (LLMs), autonomous agents have emerged as a powerful paradigm for achieving general intelligence. These agents dynamically leverage tools, memory, and reasoning capabilities to accomplish user-defined goals. As agent systems grow in complexity, agent workflows—structured orchestration frameworks have become central to enabling scalable, controllable, and secure AI behaviors. This survey provides a comprehensive review of agent workflow systems, spanning academ
papers.contribution: Provides a comprehensive survey of agent workflow design, covering workflow representations, optimization techniques, and evaluation methodologies.
papers.limitation: Survey paper; does not propose new methods. Coverage of adaptive/dynamic workflow selection is limited.

papers.title: HiVA: Self-organized Hierarchical Variable Agent via Goal-driven Semantic-Topological Evolution
papers.abstract: Autonomous agents play a crucial role in advancing Artificial General Intelligence, enabling problem decomposition and tool orchestration through Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing paradigms face a critical trade-off. On one hand, reusable fixed workflows require manual reconfiguration upon environmental changes; on the other hand, flexible reactive loops fail to distill reasoning progress into transferable structures. We introduce Hierarchical Variable Agent (HiVA), a novel framewo
papers.contribution: Proposes self-organized hierarchical variable agents using goal-driven semantic-topological evolution, dynamically adjusting agent hierarchies based on task goals.
papers.limitation: Hierarchical evolution adds complexity; convergence guarantees for the self-organization process are not provided.

papers.title: AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation Framework
papers.contribution: Introduces a foundational multi-agent conversation framework supporting customizable agent roles, human-in-the-loop patterns, and flexible conversation topologies for diverse LLM applications.
papers.limitation: Conversation-based coordination is fixed at design time; does not support dynamic topology switching based on task characteristics.

papers.title: ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Development
papers.venue: Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
papers.abstract: Software development is a complex task that necessitates cooperation among multiple members with diverse skills. Numerous studies used deep learning to improve specific phases in a waterfall model, such as design, coding, and testing. However, the deep learning model in each phase requires unique designs, leading to technical inconsistencies across various phases, which results in a fragmented and ineffective development process. In this paper, we introduce ChatDev, a chat-powered software devel
papers.contribution: Proposes a virtual software company powered by communicative LLM agents following a waterfall development process with specialized roles (CEO, CTO, programmer, tester).
papers.limitation: Fixed waterfall topology; cannot adapt to tasks better suited for iterative or parallel development patterns.

papers.title: CAMEL: Communicative Agents for "Mind" Exploration of Large Language Model Society
papers.abstract: The rapid advancement of chat-based language models has led to remarkable progress in complex task-solving. However, their success heavily relies on human input to guide the conversation, which can be challenging and time-consuming. This paper explores the potential of building scalable techniques to facilitate autonomous cooperation among communicative agents, and provides insight into their"cognitive"processes. To address the challenges of achieving autonomous cooperation, we propose a novel c
papers.contribution: Introduces role-playing between communicative agents for autonomous cooperation, enabling exploration of multi-agent collaboration behaviors in language model societies.
papers.limitation: Two-agent role-playing limits scalability; coordination patterns beyond dyadic interaction are not explored.

papers.title: G-Designer: Architecting Multi-agent Communication Topologies via Graph Neural Networks
papers.abstract: Recent advancements in large language model (LLM)-based agents have demonstrated that collective intelligence can significantly surpass the capabilities of individual agents, primarily due to well-crafted inter-agent communication topologies. Despite the diverse and high-performing designs available, practitioners often face confusion when selecting the most effective pipeline for their specific task: \textit{Which topology is the best choice for my task, avoiding unnecessary communication token
papers.contribution: First work to employ graph neural networks for constructing task-specific multi-agent communication topologies, using a variational graph auto-encoder to generate query-adaptive agent connectivity. ICLR 2025 Workshop.
papers.limitation: GNN-based topology generation adds inference overhead; trained topologies may not transfer across task domains without fine-tuning.

papers.title: ReSo: A Reward-driven Self-organizing LLM-based Multi-Agent System for Reasoning Tasks
papers.venue: Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
papers.abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) have emerged as a promising approach for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models in complex problem-solving; however, current MAS frameworks suffer from poor flexibility and scalability with underdeveloped optimization strategies. To address these challenges, we propose ReSo, which integrates task graph generation with a reward-driven two-stage agent selection process centered on our Collaborative Reward Model that provides fine-grained reward sign
papers.contribution: Integrates task graph generation with reward-driven two-stage agent selection using a Collaborative Reward Model, achieving strong results on math and science MAS benchmarks. Published at EMNLP 2025.
papers.limitation: Reward model training requires task-specific annotation; generalization to open-ended tasks without clear reward signals is untested.

papers.title: Controlling Performance and Budget of a Centralized Multi-agent LLM System with Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit complementary strengths across domains and come with varying inference costs, motivating the design of multi-agent LLM systems where specialized models collaborate efficiently. Existing approaches predominantly rely on decentralized frameworks, which invoke multiple LLMs for every input and thus lead to substantial and uncontrolled inference costs. In this work, we introduce a centralized multi-LLM framework, where a controller LLM selectively coordinates a p
papers.contribution: Formulates centralized multi-LLM coordination as RL with dual objectives (performance and cost), proposing CoRL that enables controllable multi-budget operation modes.
papers.limitation: Centralized controller does not scale well to large agent pools; budget control granularity is coarse.

papers.title: Cut the Crap: An Economical Communication Pipeline for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
papers.abstract: Recent advancements in large language model (LLM)-powered agents have shown that collective intelligence can significantly outperform individual capabilities, largely attributed to the meticulously designed inter-agent communication topologies. Though impressive in performance, existing multi-agent pipelines inherently introduce substantial token overhead, as well as increased economic costs, which pose challenges for their large-scale deployments. In response to this challenge, we propose an ec
papers.contribution: Identifies and formalizes communication redundancy in LLM multi-agent systems, performing one-shot pruning on spatial-temporal message-passing graphs to achieve 28-73% token reduction while maintaining performance.
papers.limitation: One-shot pruning is static; does not adapt pruning decisions to individual queries or evolving task states.

papers.title: AgentDropout: Dynamic Agent Elimination for Token-Efficient and High-Performance LLM-Based Multi-Agent Collaboration
papers.venue: Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
papers.abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) based on large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in collaborative problem-solving. However, they still face substantial challenges of low communication efficiency and suboptimal task performance, making the careful design of the agents’ communication topologies particularly important. Inspired by the management theory that roles in an efficient team are often dynamically adjusted, we propose AgentDropout , which identifies redundant agents a
papers.contribution: Proposes dynamic agent elimination during multi-agent collaboration, learning to drop unnecessary agents for token efficiency while preserving high performance. Published at ACL 2025.
papers.limitation: Agent dropout decisions are made at the start; does not support mid-execution agent reintroduction if initial dropout was too aggressive.

papers.title: A survey on LLM-based multi-agent systems: workflow, infrastructure, and challenges
papers.abstract: The pursuit of more intelligent and credible autonomous systems, akin to human society, has been a long-standing endeavor for humans. Leveraging the exceptional reasoning and planning capabilities of large language models (LLMs), LLM-based agents have been proposed and have achieved remarkable success across a wide array of tasks. Notably, LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) are considered a promising pathway towards realizing general artificial intelligence that is equivalent to or surpasses hu
papers.contribution: Surveys multi-agent system design with focus on workflow infrastructure and operational challenges, providing practical guidance for system builders.
papers.limitation: Infrastructure focus; theoretical foundations for adaptive orchestration are not deeply explored.

papers.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Language models are increasingly being deployed for general problem solving across a wide range of tasks, but are still confined to token-level, left-to-right decision-making processes during inference. This means they can fall short in tasks that require exploration, strategic lookahead, or where initial decisions play a pivotal role. To surmount these challenges, we introduce a new framework for language model inference, Tree of Thoughts (ToT), which generalizes over the popular Chain of Thoug
papers.contribution: Extends chain-of-thought to tree-structured exploration with backtracking, enabling deliberate problem-solving through systematic search over thought sequences.
papers.limitation: Computational cost scales with tree breadth and depth; single-agent framework without multi-agent coordination.

papers.title: The Rise and Potential of Large Language Model Based Agents: A Survey
papers.abstract: For a long time, humanity has pursued artificial intelligence (AI) equivalent to or surpassing the human level, with AI agents considered a promising vehicle for this pursuit. AI agents are artificial entities that sense their environment, make decisions, and take actions. Many efforts have been made to develop intelligent agents, but they mainly focus on advancement in algorithms or training strategies to enhance specific capabilities or performance on particular tasks. Actually, what the commu
papers.contribution: Provides a comprehensive survey of LLM-based agents covering perception, brain, and action modules, establishing a canonical taxonomy for agent architectures with 1500+ citations.
papers.limitation: Published in 2023; does not cover the rapid developments in multi-agent orchestration and adaptive systems from 2024-2026.

papers.title: A Survey on LLM-based Multi-Agent System: Recent Advances and New Frontiers in Application
papers.abstract: LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems ( LLM-MAS ) have become a research hotspot since the rise of large language models (LLMs). However, with the continuous influx of new related works, the existing reviews struggle to capture them comprehensively. This paper presents a comprehensive survey of these studies. We first discuss the definition of LLM-MAS, a framework encompassing much of previous work. We provide an overview of the various applications of LLM-MAS in (i) solving complex tasks, (ii) simulati
papers.contribution: Surveys recent advances in LLM-based multi-agent systems covering communication protocols, coordination mechanisms, and applications across multiple domains.
papers.limitation: Broad coverage trades off depth; adaptive orchestration switching is mentioned but not deeply analyzed.

papers.title: Pyramid MoA: A Probabilistic Framework for Cost-Optimized Anytime Inference
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) face a persistent trade-off between inference cost and reasoning capability. While"Oracle"models (e.g., Llama-3-70B) achieve state-of-the-art accuracy, they are prohibitively expensive for high-volume deployment. Smaller models (e.g., 8B parameters) are cost-effective but struggle with complex tasks. In this work, we propose"Pyramid MoA", a hierarchical Mixture-of-Agents architecture that uses a lightweight Router to dynamically escalate queries only when necessary.
papers.contribution: Proposes a probabilistic framework for cost-optimized anytime inference using a pyramid of mixture-of-agents layers, enabling early stopping when confidence is sufficient.
papers.limitation: Pyramid structure is fixed at design time; does not adapt the number or arrangement of layers based on task characteristics.

papers.title: Chain of Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
papers.abstract: We explore how generating a chain of thought -- a series of intermediate reasoning steps -- significantly improves the ability of large language models to perform complex reasoning. In particular, we show how such reasoning abilities emerge naturally in sufficiently large language models via a simple method called chain of thought prompting, where a few chain of thought demonstrations are provided as exemplars in prompting. Experiments on three large language models show that chain of thought pr
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that providing step-by-step reasoning examples enables LLMs to perform complex multi-step reasoning, establishing a foundational technique used across all agent systems.
papers.limitation: Single-agent technique; does not address how to distribute reasoning steps across multiple agents or orchestration patterns.

papers.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
papers.abstract: While large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities across tasks in language understanding and interactive decision making, their abilities for reasoning (e.g. chain-of-thought prompting) and acting (e.g. action plan generation) have primarily been studied as separate topics. In this paper, we explore the use of LLMs to generate both reasoning traces and task-specific actions in an interleaved manner, allowing for greater synergy between the two: reasoning traces help th
papers.contribution: Unifies reasoning (chain-of-thought) and acting (tool use) in a single agent loop, establishing the foundational reasoning-acting paradigm used in modern agent systems.
papers.limitation: Single-agent paradigm; does not address coordination between multiple ReAct agents or when to use simpler approaches.

papers.title: Reflexion: language agents with verbal reinforcement learning
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have been increasingly used to interact with external environments (e.g., games, compilers, APIs) as goal-driven agents. However, it remains challenging for these language agents to quickly and efficiently learn from trial-and-error as traditional reinforcement learning methods require extensive training samples and expensive model fine-tuning. We propose Reflexion, a novel framework to reinforce language agents not by updating weights, but instead through linguistic
papers.contribution: Introduces verbal self-reflection as a reinforcement mechanism for language agents, enabling learning from mistakes through natural language feedback rather than gradient updates.
papers.limitation: Self-reflection quality depends on the model's ability to diagnose its own errors; may amplify biases in the base model.

papers.title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
papers.abstract: Language models (LMs) exhibit remarkable abilities to solve new tasks from just a few examples or textual instructions, especially at scale. They also, paradoxically, struggle with basic functionality, such as arithmetic or factual lookup, where much simpler and smaller models excel. In this paper, we show that LMs can teach themselves to use external tools via simple APIs and achieve the best of both worlds. We introduce Toolformer, a model trained to decide which APIs to call, when to call the
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that language models can learn to decide when and how to use external tools through self-supervised training, establishing tool-augmented LLMs as a paradigm.
papers.limitation: Tool selection is learned per-model; does not address orchestration of tool use across multiple agents.

papers.title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
papers.abstract: We introduce Voyager, the first LLM-powered embodied lifelong learning agent in Minecraft that continuously explores the world, acquires diverse skills, and makes novel discoveries without human intervention. Voyager consists of three key components: 1) an automatic curriculum that maximizes exploration, 2) an ever-growing skill library of executable code for storing and retrieving complex behaviors, and 3) a new iterative prompting mechanism that incorporates environment feedback, execution err
papers.contribution: Introduces a lifelong learning agent that uses automatic curriculum, skill library, and iterative prompting for open-ended exploration in Minecraft, demonstrating continual agent improvement.
papers.limitation: Single-agent system; curriculum and skill management do not consider multi-agent coordination or task delegation.

papers.title: DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into State-of-the-Art Pipelines
papers.contribution: Introduces a programming framework that compiles declarative language model pipelines into optimized prompts, enabling systematic prompt and pipeline optimization.
papers.limitation: Optimization is offline; does not support runtime adaptation of pipeline structure based on input characteristics.

papers.title: EcoAssistant: Using LLM Assistant More Affordably and Accurately
papers.abstract: Today, users ask Large language models (LLMs) as assistants to answer queries that require external knowledge; they ask about the weather in a specific city, about stock prices, and even about where specific locations are within their neighborhood. These queries require the LLM to produce code that invokes external APIs to answer the user's question, yet LLMs rarely produce correct code on the first try, requiring iterative code refinement upon execution results. In addition, using LLM assistant
papers.contribution: Proposes a cost-aware assistant that combines code-driven and LLM-driven approaches to minimize API costs while maintaining accuracy, introducing a solution hierarchy for cost optimization.
papers.limitation: Solution hierarchy is manually defined; does not learn optimal cost-quality tradeoffs from data.

papers.title: Mixture-of-Agents Enhances Large Language Model Capabilities
papers.abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) demonstrate substantial capabilities in natural language understanding and generation tasks. With the growing number of LLMs, how to harness the collective expertise of multiple LLMs is an exciting open direction. Toward this goal, we propose a new approach that leverages the collective strengths of multiple LLMs through a Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) methodology. In our approach, we construct a layered MoA architecture wherein each layer comprises mult
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that layered mixtures of LLM agents, where each layer refines outputs from the previous layer, can significantly surpass individual model capabilities.
papers.limitation: Fixed layered topology; all agents process all inputs regardless of difficulty, leading to unnecessary computation for simple queries.

papers.title: More Agents Is All You Need
papers.abstract: We find that, simply via a sampling-and-voting method, the performance of large language models (LLMs) scales with the number of agents instantiated. Also, this method, termed as Agent Forest, is orthogonal to existing complicated methods to further enhance LLMs, while the degree of enhancement is correlated to the task difficulty. We conduct comprehensive experiments on a wide range of LLM benchmarks to verify the presence of our finding, and to study the properties that can facilitate its occu
papers.contribution: Shows that simply scaling the number of LLM agents with majority voting can match or exceed more sophisticated multi-agent strategies, establishing a strong baseline for agent scaling.
papers.limitation: Brute-force scaling is cost-inefficient; does not address when fewer agents with better coordination would suffice.

papers.title: HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face
papers.abstract: Solving complicated AI tasks with different domains and modalities is a key step toward artificial general intelligence. While there are numerous AI models available for various domains and modalities, they cannot handle complicated AI tasks autonomously. Considering large language models (LLMs) have exhibited exceptional abilities in language understanding, generation, interaction, and reasoning, we advocate that LLMs could act as a controller to manage existing AI models to solve complicated A
papers.contribution: Uses ChatGPT as a controller to manage and coordinate specialized AI models from Hugging Face, demonstrating task planning and model selection for complex multi-modal tasks.
papers.limitation: Single-controller bottleneck; model selection is based on descriptions rather than empirical performance, leading to suboptimal choices.

papers.title: LLM-Blender: Ensembling Large Language Models with Pairwise Ranking and Generative Fusion
papers.venue: Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
papers.abstract: We present LLM-Blender, an ensembling framework designed to attain consistently superior performance by leveraging the diverse strengths of multiple open-source large language models (LLMs). Our framework consists of two modules: PairRanker and GenFuser, addressing the observation that optimal LLMs for different examples can significantly vary. PairRanker employs a specialized pairwise comparison method to distinguish subtle differences between candidate outputs. It jointly encodes the input tex
papers.contribution: Proposes pairwise ranking and generative fusion to ensemble outputs from multiple LLMs, learning to combine model strengths through a trained ranking module.
papers.limitation: Requires running all candidate models for every input; no routing to avoid unnecessary computation.

papers.title: A Dynamic LLM-Powered Agent Network for Task-Oriented Agent Collaboration
papers.abstract: Recent studies show that collaborating multiple large language model (LLM) powered agents is a promising way for task solving. However, current approaches are constrained by using a fixed number of agents and static communication structures. In this work, we propose automatically selecting a team of agents from candidates to collaborate in a dynamic communication structure toward different tasks and domains. Specifically, we build a framework named Dynamic LLM-Powered Agent Network ($\textbf{DyL
papers.contribution: Proposes DyLAN, automatically selecting agent teams from candidates using an Agent Importance Score for task-specific collaboration in dynamic communication structures.
papers.limitation: Agent selection is based on importance scoring which may not capture complementary skill combinations; communication structure is dynamic but unstructured.

papers.title: Hybrid LLM: Cost-Efficient and Quality-Aware Query Routing
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) excel in most NLP tasks but also require expensive cloud servers for deployment due to their size, while smaller models that can be deployed on lower cost (e.g., edge) devices, tend to lag behind in terms of response quality. Therefore in this work we propose a hybrid inference approach which combines their respective strengths to save cost and maintain quality. Our approach uses a router that assigns queries to the small or large model based on the predicted query d
papers.contribution: Proposes a cost-efficient query routing system that combines a small local model with a large cloud model, using a learned router to minimize API costs while maintaining quality.
papers.limitation: Binary local/cloud routing; does not consider multi-tier cascades or multi-agent coordination patterns.

clusters.name: adaptive-orchestration-switching
clusters.description: Systems that dynamically select or switch between different orchestration patterns (sequential, parallel, hierarchical, debate) based on task characteristics at runtime

clusters.name: llm-routing-cascading
clusters.description: Systems for routing queries to appropriate LLMs or cascading through model tiers based on task difficulty, optimizing cost-quality tradeoffs

clusters.name: topology-learning-optimization
clusters.description: Methods for learning, designing, or optimizing the communication topology and agent connectivity graph in multi-agent systems

clusters.name: workflow-optimization
clusters.description: Automated optimization, generation, and evolution of agentic workflows and pipelines

clusters.name: multi-agent-frameworks
clusters.description: Foundational multi-agent frameworks and systems for LLM-based collaboration

clusters.name: agent-reasoning-foundations
clusters.description: Foundational reasoning and planning techniques that underlie agent capabilities

clusters.name: surveys-benchmarks
clusters.description: Survey papers and benchmark frameworks providing comprehensive overviews of multi-agent systems and LLM agents

exclusion_reasons: Non-LLM/NLP domain (control theory, robotics, wireless, medical)
Domain application papers without methodological contribution to orchestration
